questions.explanation: Independent events: $P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$.
steps: **Independent events** (one doesn't affect the other) → multiply for AND.
$P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$.

questions.promptTex: \text{For mutually exclusive events: } P(A \text{ or } B) = ?
hints: Mutually exclusive → no overlap → just add for OR.
questions.explanation: Mutually exclusive events have no overlap, so $P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B)$.
steps: Mutually exclusive: cannot both happen, $P(A \text{ and } B) = 0$.
OR rule: $P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) + P(B) - 0 = P(A) + P(B)$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Flip a coin and roll a die.} \\ P(\text{H and 4}) = ? \text{ (round to 3 decimals)}
steps: Coin and die are independent.
$P(\text{H and 4}) = P(\text{H}) \cdot P(\text{4}) = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{1}{12}$.
Decimal: $\approx 0.083$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Bag: 3 red, 5 blue marbles. Draw 2 } \textbf{without replacement}. \\ P(\text{both red}) = ?
hints: Without replacement: $P(\text{1st R}) \cdot P(\text{2nd R} | \text{1st R})$.
steps: Without replacement → second draw depends on first.
$P(\text{first R}) = 3/8$.
After taking one R, only 2 R left out of 7 total: $P(\text{second R} \mid \text{first R}) = 2/7$.
$P(\text{both R}) = \dfrac{3}{8} \cdot \dfrac{2}{7} = \dfrac{6}{56} = \dfrac{3}{28}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Bag: 3 red, 5 blue. Draw 2 } \textbf{with replacement}. \\ P(\text{both red}) = ?
steps: With replacement → independent (probabilities reset).
$P(\text{R}) = 3/8$ each draw.
$P(\text{both R}) = \dfrac{3}{8} \cdot \dfrac{3}{8} = \dfrac{9}{64}$.
(Compare: with replacement gives a higher probability than without — the second draw isn't 'used up'.)

questions.promptTex: \text{Roll a die. } P(\text{2 or 5}) = ? \text{ (decimal, round to 3 places)}
hints: Mutually exclusive (can't roll both at once on one die).
steps: Rolling a 2 and rolling a 5 on a single die are mutually exclusive.

questions.promptTex: \text{Card from a 52-deck.} \\ P(\text{heart or face card}) = ? \text{ (round to 3 decimals)}
hints: NOT mutually exclusive! Use general OR: $P(H) + P(F) - P(H \cap F)$.
questions.explanation: $13/52 + 12/52 - 3/52 = 22/52 \approx 0.423$ (subtract overlap of J, Q, K of hearts).
steps: $P(\text{heart}) = 13/52$. $P(\text{face card}) = 12/52$ (J, Q, K of each of 4 suits).
Overlap: J, Q, K of hearts = 3 cards. $P(\text{heart and face}) = 3/52$.
$\approx 0.423$.
All 4 flips independent: multiply.

questions.promptTex: \text{Flip a coin 4 times. } P(\text{at least one tail}) = ? \text{ (decimal, 4 decimals)}
steps: Complement: $P(\text{at least one T}) = 1 - P(\text{no T}) = 1 - P(\text{all H})$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Are 'rolling a 2' and 'rolling an even number' mutually exclusive?}
hints: Rolling a 2 IS rolling an even number.
questions.explanation: Rolling a 2 satisfies BOTH events. Not mutually exclusive.
steps: Mutually exclusive = cannot both happen.
If you roll a 2, then 'roll a 2' and 'roll an even number' both happen simultaneously.
**Not mutually exclusive.**

questions.promptTex: \text{Bag: 4 red, 6 blue. Draw 3 with replacement.} \\ P(\text{exactly 3 reds}) = ? \text{ (decimal, 4 places)}
steps: With replacement → each draw independent, $P(\text{R}) = 4/10 = 0.4$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Card from a deck.} \\ P(\text{red card or queen}) = ? \text{ (round to 3 decimals)}
steps: $P(\text{red}) = 26/52$ (hearts + diamonds).
$P(\text{queen}) = 4/52$ (one in each suit).
Overlap (red queens): 2/52 (queen of hearts, queen of diamonds).

questions.prompt: For ANY two events A and B (independent or not), what is the correct formula for P(A and B)?
choices: P(A) · P(B | A) — the GENERAL multiplication rule
hints: $P(A) \cdot P(B)$ only works for INDEPENDENT events.
The general formula uses CONDITIONAL probability $P(B \mid A)$.
questions.explanation: The general multiplication rule: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$. The $P(B \mid A)$ ('probability of B given A') captures whether A's occurrence affects B. When events are *independent*, $P(B \mid A) = P(B)$ and the formula reduces to the familiar $P(A) \cdot P(B)$.
steps: **Universal vs special case.** The general multiplication rule works for ANY two events: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$.
**What is $P(B \mid A)$?** 'Probability of $B$ GIVEN that $A$ has occurred.' It re-scopes the sample space to outcomes where $A$ happened.
**Special case — independence.** Events are independent ⇔ $P(B \mid A) = P(B)$ (knowing $A$ doesn't change $B$'s probability). Then $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$.
**Why this generalizes.** With replacement → independent → multiply. Without replacement → dependent → must use $P(B \mid A)$. Same single formula handles both.

**Pattern lesson.** Whenever the second event's probability *depends* on the first, use $P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$. This is the bridge from compound events to *conditional probability* — the next topic.

questions.promptTex: \text{Roll a fair die } 3 \text{ times.} \\ P(\text{all three rolls are different}) = ? \\ \text{Round to 3 decimals.}
hints: Second roll must differ from first: $5/6$.
Third roll must differ from first two: $4/6$.
questions.explanation: Roll-by-roll: the first roll can be anything ($p = 1$). The second must differ from the first ($5$ of $6$ values work, $p = 5/6$). The third must differ from the first two ($4$ of $6$ values, $p = 4/6$). Multiply: $1 \cdot (5/6) \cdot (4/6) = 20/36 = 5/9 \approx 0.556$.
steps: **Recognize the type — sequential compound event.** Three rolls in order; condition successively on each previous roll.
**Roll 1: any value.** $p_1 = 1$ (no constraint yet).
**Roll 2: must differ from roll 1.** $5$ of $6$ values work. $p_2 = 5/6$.
**Roll 3: must differ from rolls 1 and 2.** $4$ of $6$ values work. $p_3 = 4/6$.
**Multiply (chain rule).** $P(\text{all different}) = 1 \cdot \dfrac{5}{6} \cdot \dfrac{4}{6} = \dfrac{20}{36} = \dfrac{5}{9} \approx 0.556$.

**Sanity check.** Roughly half — feels right for 3 rolls with 6 possible values. (Compare: 4 rolls all different $= 1 \cdot 5/6 \cdot 4/6 \cdot 3/6 \approx 0.278$; 5 rolls $\approx 0.0926$; 7 rolls is impossible since only 6 distinct values exist.)

questions.promptTex: \text{Flip a fair coin } 5 \text{ times.} \\ P(\text{exactly 3 heads}) = ? \\ \text{Round to 4 decimals.}
hints: Choose which 3 of 5 flips are heads: $\binom{5}{3} = 10$ ways.
questions.explanation: Two ingredients: (1) count the number of arrangements with exactly 3 heads = $\binom{5}{3} = 10$. (2) each such arrangement has probability $(1/2)^5 = 1/32$. Multiply: $10/32 = 5/16 = 0.3125$. This is the binomial formula in action.
steps: **Recognize the type — binomial.** We want exactly $k$ successes (heads) in $n$ independent trials. Two ingredients: how many ways the successes can be arranged, and the probability of each arrangement.
**Count arrangements.** $\binom{5}{3} = 10$ ways to choose which 3 of the 5 flips are heads: $\{HHHTT, HHTHT, HHTTH, HTHHT, HTHTH, HTTHH, THHHT, THHTH, THTHH, TTHHH\}$.
**Probability of each arrangement.** Each specific sequence of 3 H's and 2 T's has probability $(1/2)^3 \cdot (1/2)^2 = (1/2)^5 = 1/32$.

**General binomial.** $P(\text{exactly } k \text{ heads in } n \text{ flips}) = \binom{n}{k} \cdot p^k \cdot (1-p)^{n-k}$ where $p$ is the per-flip head probability. This formula governs an enormous range of phenomena: defective items in batches, survey responses, particle decay.

questions.promptTex: \text{Draw } 5 \text{ cards from a deck, WITH replacement.} \\ P(\text{at least one ace}) = ? \\ \text{Round to 4 decimals.}
hints: Use complement: $1 - P(\text{no aces in 5 draws})$.
questions.explanation: Complement strategy. $P(\text{not ace per draw}) = 48/52 = 12/13$. With replacement → independent. $P(\text{no aces in 5}) = (12/13)^5 \approx 0.6717$. So $P(\geq 1\, \text{ace}) = 1 - 0.6717 \approx 0.3283$.
steps: **Recognize the type — 'at least one'.** Complement is much easier than directly enumerating 1 ace, 2 aces, 3 aces, 4 aces, 5 aces.
**Complement.** $P(\geq 1\,\text{ace}) = 1 - P(\text{no aces in any of 5 draws})$.
**Per-draw 'not ace'.** $48$ non-aces out of $52$ cards: $P(\text{not ace}) = 48/52 = 12/13$.
**Five independent draws (with replacement).** $P(\text{no aces in 5}) = (12/13)^5 \approx 0.6717$.
**Take complement.** $P(\geq 1\,\text{ace}) = 1 - 0.6717 \approx 0.3283$.
**Pattern lesson — 'at least one' problems.** Almost always solve via complement: $P(\geq 1) = 1 - P(0)$. Direct enumeration multiplies the work by many cases; complement collapses it to one.

questions.promptTex: \text{Three INDEPENDENT events each have probability } 0.7. \\ P(\text{all three happen}) = ?
questions.explanation: For independent events, $P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C) = 0.7^3 = 0.343$.
steps: **Independence ⇒ multiply.** For independent $A, B, C$: $P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C)$.

**Compare to a single event.** Each event alone has probability $0.7$ (likely). But all three happening drops to $0.343$ — less than half. Independent events compound multiplicatively, and probabilities below 1 shrink fast.

**Real-world intuition.** Reliability engineering: a system needs three independent components to succeed (each with $70\%$ reliability), system reliability is $34.3\%$. To get high system reliability, you need REDUNDANCY, not more independent steps.

questions.promptTex: \text{A random 4-bit binary string (each bit independently 0 or 1 with probability 1/2) is chosen.} \\ P(\text{at least 2 ones}) = ? \\ \text{Give an exact decimal.}
steps: **Recognize the type.** Counting strings with at least $k$ ones — easier to exclude the small cases ($0$ ones, $1$ one) and complement.
**Total possibilities.** $2^4 = 16$ equally likely 4-bit strings.
**Count exclusions.** $0$ ones: just $\{0000\}$, 1 outcome. $1$ one: pick which position is the $1$ — $\binom{4}{1} = 4$ outcomes.
**Subtract from 1.** $P(\geq 2\, \text{ones}) = 1 - \dfrac{1 + 4}{16} = \dfrac{11}{16} = 0.6875$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Bag: 3 red, 5 blue. Draw 2 WITHOUT replacement.} \\ P(\text{at least one red}) = ? \\ \text{Round to 3 decimals.}
questions.explanation: Complement: $1 - P(\text{both blue})$. Without replacement, $P(\text{both blue}) = \dfrac{5}{8} \cdot \dfrac{4}{7} = \dfrac{20}{56} = \dfrac{5}{14}$. So $P(\geq 1\, R) = 1 - 5/14 = 9/14 \approx 0.643$.
steps: **Recognize 'at least one' → complement.** $P(\geq 1\, R) = 1 - P(\text{no reds in 2 draws}) = 1 - P(\text{both blue})$.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="30" cy="110" r="6" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="24" y="104" font-size="10" fill="#0f172a" font-weight="600" text-anchor="end">start</text><line x1="36" y1="107" x2="100" y2="60" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="36" y1="113" x2="100" y2="160" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="108" cy="55" r="6" fill="#dc2626"/><circle cx="108" cy="165" r="6" fill="#2563eb"/><text x="60" y="75" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-weight="700">3/8 R</text><text x="60" y="148" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb" font-weight="700">5/8 B</text><line x1="114" y1="50" x2="200" y2="30" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="114" y1="60" x2="200" y2="85" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="114" y1="160" x2="200" y2="135" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="114" y1="170" x2="200" y2="195" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="144" y="32" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626" font-weight="600">2/7 R</text><text x="144" y="83" font-size="10" fill="#2563eb" font-weight="600">5/7 B</text><text x="144" y="135" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626" font-weight="600">3/7 R</text><text x="144" y="199" font-size="10" fill="#2563eb" font-weight="600">4/7 B</text><text x="208" y="34" font-size="10" fill="#0f172a">RR: 6/56</text><text x="208" y="87" font-size="10" fill="#0f172a">RB: 15/56</text><text x="208" y="137" font-size="10" fill="#0f172a">BR: 15/56</text><text x="208" y="201" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a" font-weight="700">BB: 20/56</text><text x="12" y="215" font-size="10" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">conditional probabilities change after first draw (no replacement)</text></svg>

steps: **Compute $P(\text{both blue})$ — the chain rule.** $P(\text{1st B}) = 5/8$. After removing one blue, the bag has $3$ R and $4$ B (total $7$). $P(\text{2nd B} \mid \text{1st B}) = 4/7$. Multiply: $P(\text{both B}) = \dfrac{5}{8} \cdot \dfrac{4}{7} = \dfrac{20}{56} = \dfrac{5}{14}$.
**Take complement.** $P(\geq 1\, R) = 1 - \dfrac{5}{14} = \dfrac{9}{14} \approx 0.643$.

**Why complement is easier than direct.** Direct: $P(\geq 1\, R) = P(\text{exactly 1 R}) + P(\text{exactly 2 R}) = (RR) + (RB) + (BR) = 6/56 + 15/56 + 15/56 = 36/56 = 9/14$ ✓. Three cases vs. one — complement wins.

**Pattern lesson.** The 'both blue' chain rule is the same general multiplication rule from Q13: $P(B_1 \cap B_2) = P(B_1) \cdot P(B_2 \mid B_1)$. Without replacement → dependent → must use the conditional form.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two fair dice are rolled.} \\ P(\text{sum is at least 9}) = ? \\ \text{Round to 3 decimals.}
hints: Count ordered pairs $(i, j)$ with $i + j \geq 9$.
steps: **Use the 6×6 grid sample space.** All 36 ordered pairs equally likely.

stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><text x="30" y="203" font-size="11" fill="#16a34a" font-style="italic">10 of 36 cells have sum ≥ 9</text></svg>

steps: **Probability.** $P = 10/36 = 5/18 \approx 0.278$.

**Symmetric check.** By symmetry, $P(\text{sum} \leq 5) = P(\text{sum} \geq 9) = 10/36$ (the distribution is symmetric around the median 7). And $P(\text{sum} = 7) = 6/36$, plus $P(\text{sum} = 6\,\text{or}\, 8) = 10/36$. Verify: $10 + 10 + 6 + 10 = 36$ ✓.
